title: vimeo_1187475985
displayTitle: 2026-04-29 - Social Services and Community Committee - Ministry for Pacific Peoples, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025
topic: Ministry for Pacific Peoples, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025
description: Ministry for Pacific Peoples, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025 Ministry for Pacific Peoples Gerardine Clifford-Lidstone, Secretary, Ministry for Pacific Peoples Bernadette Scanlon, Deputy Secretary, Policy and Insights, Ministry for Pacific Peoples
descriptionSummary: Ministry for Pacific Peoples, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025. Ministry for Pacific Peoples. Gerardine Clifford-Lidstone, Secretary, Ministry for Pacific Peoples

chunks.text: Good afternoon and welcome to the Social Services and Select Committee. So we have the Ministry for Pacific Peoples this afternoon to give us some information regarding that Long-term Insights briefing. And I think we have Jordane and Bernadette here with us. We've got a 45-minute hearing. Time is yours. If you want to leave some time towards the end for questions, please feel welcome to do so.

Fa'afetai lava, and thank you for the opportunity to appear before you today. Firstly, if I can just introduce Bernadette Scanlon, our Deputy Secretary of Policy and Insights, who will be responding with me today. And if you just bear with me, I just have some opening comments, which will take about 10 minutes. So can I just begin by acknowledging the enduring kinship ties that bind Maori and Pacific Peoples in this nation. These are not new connections formed by migration alone.

They are ancient relationships shaped by navigation, shared cosmologies, and here in New Zealand, as early as the 1920s and 1930s, by the long-standing role of iwi in welcoming and hosting and looking after Pacific families as they made their homes on these shores and within the boundaries of various iwi around the country.

There is no other country in the world that can claim this strong kinship link with Pacific nations. Through the development of this briefing, we heard many stories of iwi exercising their stewardship role, extending their resources, their manaakitanga, and their services to ensure Pacific peoples and families were cared for. These stories matter because they remind us that Pacific settlement in New Zealand has always been relational, grounded in whakapapa, place, and mutual responsibility.

Over generations, these relationships have deepened into marriages, shared communities, shared churches, shared schools, shared struggles, and increasingly, shared opportunities have produced a growing cohort of people who claim rightly and proudly kinship ties to both Maori and Pacific worlds.

This is not a marginal phenomenon. It's a distinctive and consequential demographic shift underway in New Zealand. The Long-term Insights briefing deliberately places a ring around this population, not because they are separate, but because ethnic classification can render them statistically invisible, even as their influence becomes increasingly visible in our communities, our classrooms, and our workplaces. If we want good long-term policy, we need to be honest about who's coming through the gate.

And what the data is telling us is striking. The average age of people who identify with both Maori and Pacific whakapapa is only 15 years of age. It compares with Maori at 26 years of age and Pasifika at 24 years of age, and then non-Maori and non-Pacific, 38 years of age. So the Maori Pacific demographic is half the age of the non-Maori, non-Pacific population.

In demographic terms, this is not simply a young population, it's a very young population. Many are children now, and nearly all are New Zealand's future workforce. We shouldn't be surprised that they are predominantly based in our major urban areas, Auckland most prominently, but also Wellington, Christchurch, and Dunedin, while at the same time establishing growing footprints in rural and regional areas such as Northland, Waikato, South Canterbury, Otago, and Invercargill.

This combination matters. It positions this cohort at the intersection of both our urban productivity engines and our regional revitalization efforts. So what does this mean for New Zealand's future productivity and contribution to the workforce? Firstly, quite simply, they will be essential. As our broader population ages, labor force growth will increasingly depend on Maori and Pacific Peoples, and particularly on those with dual whakapapa who are coming of age in significant numbers.

This group will shoulder a growing share of workforce participation, the tax base, the care economy, and the creative industries. The question is not whether they will contribute, but whether our systems will be ready to support them to contribute at their full potential. Secondly, this cohort brings unique strengths that are highly aligned with the future of work.Many are inherently bicultural or multicultural navigators. They are accustomed to operating across worlds, to our Māori and Pacific worlds, home and institution, whenua and city.

In a labor market increasingly shaped by collaboration, relational skills, adaptability, and cross-cultural competence, these capabilities are not nice to have. They are productivity assets. But, and this is where the briefing is deliberately honest, there are also future challenges if we get this wrong.

We are already seeing persistent disparities in educational attainment, housing security, health outcomes, and exposure to the justice system for Māori and Pacific peoples. If policy settings fail to recognize the compound realities of young people with dual identity, we risk reproducing those inequities into the next generation at considerable social and fiscal cost.

There is also a classification challenge. When people are required to choose a single ethnicity, the lived experience of people can be flattened, and the policy responses can miss the mark. Education pathways, employment services, and wellbeing supports are often designed for one identity or another, when the reality for many of these young people is often both and sometimes neither comfortably.

Yet, the opportunities are just as real as the risks. We can already see amazing glimpses of what this kind of partnership can look like in practice. The relationship between the Central Pacific Collective and Ngāti Toa Rangatira provides a tangible example through the partnership initiatives such as Our Whare Our fale, where mana whenua have made available their land for a home ownership approach focused on the delivery of warm, healthy, long-term homes. Homes designed for Pacific people to put down their roots rather than cycle through

insecurity. These are not homes built in isolation. They are deliberately located near schools, public transport, health services, and community supports, recognizing that stable housing, access to education, and proximity to care are deeply interconnected. Ngāti Toa Rangatira's role as mana whenua brings a strong place-based lens and intergenerational stewardship, while the Central Pacific Collective contributes deep Pacific-led insight into whanau needs, affordability pressures, and cultural patterns of living.

The project also takes an inclusive social value approach to delivery, strengthening access to employment and business opportunities, and helping ensure the economic benefits of development are shared with the communities most affected by housing need. This approach supports capabilities, skills, and long-term resilience while remaining open, transparent, and focused on achieving the best outcomes for whanau communities and the wider system.

Together, this approach demonstrates what is possible when iwi leadership and resources and Pacific community capability are aligned, creating housing that supports wellbeing, educational continuity, and workforce participation over the long term rather than short-term fixes. For many young people with dual Māori and Pacific whakapapa, iwi is already part of their lived reality through whenua, marae, education, faith, and whanau connections.

Creating space for iwi to engage more closely in conversations about education pathways, employment, wellbeing, and place-based development offers an opportunity to ground national policy and local knowledge, cultural continuity, and intergenerational wisdom.

Just as importantly, this cohort offers New Zealand a chance to foster greater understanding across our communities. Younger people who move with confidence between different worlds have the potential to act in a global sense as translators and bridge builders, strengthening social cohesion, challenging false binaries, and modeling a more relational way of being a global citizen.

Supporting this does not dilute identity. It deepens it, positioning this briefing not simply as a reflection on Pacific futures, but as a statement of New Zealand's future, the workforce we are becoming, and our readiness to thrive in an increasingly global and diverse economy. So we offer this briefing to the committee in that spirit as an invitation to think long, think relationally, and to work alongside iwi communities and young people themselves as we lay the foundation for New Zealand's communities of tomorrow. Thank you so much for listening, and

Bernadette and I welcome questions. Thank you. The order. Thank you. Questions, members. I might kick off then as sort of comments/questions. Very interested in your point you made earlier about the connections between Māori and Pasifika. Actually, I was lucky enough to visit the Atiawa village, or a Atiawa village about two years ago in Hawaii.

And a colleague, I mean, Honorable Jenny Salisa spoke to the, uh, and, um, in her initial comments, and they, they stopped for a reason, the translation, or the translator went to translate, uh, I, I... She was either speaking in Tongan or Māori, or both, I can't remember exactly. But, uh, but they stopped.

They stopped the translator and said, "No, no, we, we understand." And it was just a, a fascinating, just that connectionSuch a long way between New Zealand and Taiwan, but it just lead me to look more closely into the Austronesian- Mm... which grows all across the Pacific- Mm... and the peoples that connects all of us. And obviously, New Zealand was the last major land mass to be settled.

Mm. But it's this Pacific-going peoples that connected through language and culture- Mm... right across the Pacific. So just a comment, and I think an important one for us to understand in this country and reflect on as we connect to the blue continent. Yes. Thank you.

Mm. Yep. Willow-Jean. Kia ora. Thank you for that. I'm particularly interested in rangatahi and unemployment. Is there anything in particular you can talk to around education, employment, pathways into employment, to that rangatahi specifically?

Sure. One of the things that was strikingly obvious to us is how young people see themselves as part of the solution, not part of the problem. And this piece, in particular, was designed to bring visibility to the two sides of the coin. Firstly, that there is a risk if we don't do anything, and secondly, the huge opportunity that comes with being more purposeful about how we design the policy.

So, the main thing here is to bring visibility because they told us directly, "We are not the problems to be fixed. We are the partners in the solution." So, this is a commitment we have to the rangatahi. Mm. And in terms of those solutions that they might be suggesting, do you have any more that you can add to that?

I think it's easy for us to focus solely on government responsibility, and I'll come to that. The thing that I found very interesting was that when our rangatahi, if they're enrolled with their iwi, the iwi collects their data as rangatahi Maori. So when we met with iwi, they were like, "Oh, but they're Maori."

Mm. So even for iwi to say, "Oh, maybe we now need to collect more information because we see everyone as Maori, and we know that they're Pacific, but they're Maori first here." So, in the first instance for the iwi that we talked to, coming to this understanding that the rangatahi have multiple identities that they may feel a strong affinity for, is really important.

In terms of the second part, government's responsibility, if agencies can't see these people- Mm... then it's very hard to develop solutions for it. So, in the first instance, while this is a think piece to encourage public conversations about long-term issues, it is also something that we will socialize with other agencies so that they understand that this dynamic does exist and look to make use of those opportunities.

Ka kite anō. Thank you. Christine. Tena koutou. Tena koutou. It's a joy to hear when you talk about dual whakapapa, those things. It's not a common statement you'll hear in a select committee. Mm. Not when our shoulders are soft. Usually, it's something else rolling up in a maybe a little bit more of a contentious issue.

But here, to hear it in such a way that you can see the future coming, it's there post in the ages, and I noticed when you talk-- And I was a little bit sort of 12 and 26 and 38, and what that is setting us up for is it is a very young population.

Mm. So how do we organize, how do we inspire, how do we resource and then get out of their way? Those sorts of things. That's a heightened interest for me. And then to that end, what are you finding or seeing or feeling from Ngati Toa down this end, and then if you reflect up to Te Tai Tokerau, so you're talking right up in now where Willow and I are from, are you finding the same sorts of things from an iwi or an hapū type engagement? Are you finding those things are happening further north?

What we found is people migrate to different areas of the country for different reasons. The challenge that we heard from participants is they say they're too Maori for their Polynesian peers and too Polynesian for their Maori peers, and it can make them feel left out.

Mm. So, I guess the visibility issue- Mm... I can't reinforce how important that is to this cohort. And it's important for all the reasons that we have discussed around productivity and contribution to our tax base when they can thrive going forward.Thank you.

Lauren. Thank you. I'm particularly interested in your insight number two, which is around the digital landscape. Mm. And I just wonder what currently are the barriers to the Māori and Pacific- Mm... children in getting into that space- Mm... and developing those skills.

I'm going to give an opportunity to Bernadette to talk. But I think in the first instance, it's different depending how old you are. Mm. So for our older generation, often it's a digital divide, having access to computers, knowing how to use computers. With our younger generation, their ability to access some kind of device- Mm... is much greater. I'll just see if you ...

Our report findings showed that there is still an access barrier, as well as capability uplift needed. There is a difference between being a digital native to being digital literate. Literate, mm. And so just because you have access to cell phones and you can use it on a daily basis doesn't mean you're going to be able to code in the future, be able to utilize AI to the optimal stage.

Mm. So it's about trying to provide the access, provide that capability uplift, and there is a desire for our Māori Pacific youth to have that. They want to be self-determinant. They want to be able to upskill and actually be digitally literate, which I think is amazing, and we need to foster. I just thought kids were born with the knowledge .

Yeah. Yeah, I might supplement, actually, because we're at a massive inflection point in terms of technology right now. We're in it. And the ability to code isn't necessarily a requirement anymore, but the ability to give the right prompts to an AI to do the coding for you is.

But it's also language and culture can help inform that. So there's the data sovereignty piece, which is probably increasingly important, but also challenging because to access these tools which are designed offshore- Mm-hmm... and the resources being built offshore don't naturally take those things into account. So this is a-- I'm not expecting any answers that sort of console all these things as everyone's grappling with this globally. I'm just really interested in what your insights are about how young people can not just be consumers but prosumers or producers.

Yep And this incredible inflection point, which everyone's grappling with here and around the world. Yeah, of course. Insight too also talks about the Pacific data sovereignty and Māori data sovereignty. And a key aspect of that is doing exactly what you said, taking ownership to develop some of the tools because if we don't, we're going to have natural biases embedded in whatever we are adopting in New Zealand.

So making sure that you've got that diversity of thought, in not just the application but in terms of the actual development and processing of, and the manufacturing of these products that we're going to be utilizing is vital to make sure that we are utilizing it best for our people. And having that diversity in the development means you remove those biases.

And if I can just add, from personal experience, the people developing our policies have huge wisdom and insight and experience, and unfortunately with that comes a lot of risk-averseness. And so, when you're dealing with 15-year-olds, their ability to find solutions for themselves is, they call it their superpower.

Mm. And if I think of my own children, for example, I saw a message pop up in my mail, and it's some app to stealth writing or something like this. So I need to have a conversation with my son about that because obviously he's needing to write something and knows that the system will be better. So how then do we keep up with our young people and with technology when we're actually on a different wave sometimes, and we come with a lot of wisdom and experience that may prevent us from making the decisions in

time for it to have a policy impact? And that age difference is so important for us to be aware of as agencies so that we can be mindful of it. I might ask one more supplement and hand over to Parameeta for another sup. But just circling back to my first comment really is, just in this incredible acceleration of technology and trying to ensure that these are New Zealand's focus and our population groups here, and that data sovereignty is important but very challenging, one to try with how quickly the technology is

changing. But is there an opportunity and/or is there any thinking around trying to include the Austronesian grouping, for want of a better term, across the Asia-Pacific, who have a lot of similar interests and concerns around exactly the same thing? The report is silent into that, but no doubt, there's opportunity to embrace that as well.

We have one initiative at the moment, the Game On initiative. You might be surprised to know that New Zealand is almost world leaders in the creation of games in the gaming industry. And we have a-Project at the moment that is working with the gaming industry, with some NGOs, and with us to provide internship opportunities for young people to learn about the opportunities, because they are so creative. And so when you give them the opportunity to focus that creativity into

an industry like gaming, then we think that this is one of the pathways that we can use. We haven't looked more broadly at the moment, but New Zealand is great at a lot of these areas that we don't amplify that often, how good we are in some of these spaces.

And we have, I think, the single biggest population groups of Polynesian peoples on the planet, right? Mm-hmm. Yes. And so we have a superpower in terms of that connectivity- Yes... to the Pacific. Maree Mitchell. Yeah. I think the superpower's old too. It's super old, like you referred to in your opening statement.

So I think it's a revitalized energy, really. But anyway, so Fiber Fale? Yes. Yes. Yeah. So you know that group. Yes. And so when we're trying to really understand more, in this regard anyway, at this committee, having people that are right on that line doing those things, young, vibrant, and are planned, and they've got an idea, and they...

I ran into them the other day at the airport. Well, they were running into me, and bang, they want to tell me. They're just so full of youthful, smart, driven people. Yes. These two young, stunning women. So I just wondered, are they a group or a firm or organization that you're connected with?

And would you recommend that we bring them in here so they could talk to us about what they think is a good future going ahead? If I may, if I just go back to your first point. One of the beautiful things about dealing with this particular cohort is how respectful they are of mātua. So they look at age with a respectful lens, and this is a very different characteristic when dealing with non-Māori, non-Pacific.

So it is about they embrace wisdom, but they also know that because they help their parents and their grandparents, that there's a big gap between what they know. So I just wanted to acknowledge the deep respect that they have for whakapapa and kinship. In terms of Fiber Fale, they do wonderful work in our community, and I've spoken with them a number of times.

And I really want to support their desire to have a really strong business offering. So they do a lot of work in the community, and they provide access to laptops, and they do AI training. They also don't want to give away their expertise for free, and they've been really clear on that.

Good. Which is absolutely what they should do. Yeah. Very good. So we will continue to navigate that space because, as you are aware, there are various procurement rules and things like that that we have to navigate with them. Sure. Thank you. Marie Christine, go ahead. Thank you. Sorry, I might have missed it in your kōrero earlier and in the report, but of that cohort, have you identified the tribal affiliations, the iwi affiliations?

Not all of them. It's been- But have you kind of got a picture of... Yes. It's complicated. Far more complicated. So iwi don't capture all. Mm. Although now they're very interested, the iwi that we talk to. The second is, depending on who the dominant person is in the household- Yeah... some of the-- And it goes back to I'm too Māori for my Polynesian peers and too Polynesian for my Māori peers.

Mm. There's a lot of interest in reconnecting those whakapapa ties. Mm. And then there's some that are really clear. They know who their iwi is, and they proudly manage both. So it's not a straightforward answer. And because you did identify where they're sort of concentrated.

Mm. And of course, I think of being a Ngāpuhi- Yeah... and one in five- Being a Ngāpuhi... are Ngāpuhi, so I'm assuming there's going to be a lot of them. If Karnelle Sefulonia's spokesperson was here, she would say she has these mokopuna Mm... who are both Pasifika and Māori- Mm... and Ngāpuhi, by the way. But I also think, I organized a whānau reunion along with my cousin back in 2006, and to our surprise, half of the whare was full of Tongan Māori relations that we didn't actually know existed until the whānau reunion.

And then you find out ancestor went to Tonga and had five wives, and there are a whole lot of descendants from that line living primarily in South Auckland, and first time really making that connection back to the marae. Mm. So yeah, I was interested in if through census, and if we're losing census, how do we try and capture this data and those connections and then support that-reconnection to and strengthening when we know identity security, that sense of identity can make such a huge

difference. Yeah, so just- Sure... wondering. Because it comes through in the report so much. So I'm like, what are the mechanisms for that? I'll get Bernadette to talk to the data, but if I can just say that one of the drivers that we found in doing this work is that when Pacific people marry outside of a Pacific culture, they marry a Maori first.

So that's the dynamic at the moment. Mm. It may change in the future. But up until the point that we did this, Pacific people marrying outside Pacific will more likely marry a Maori next. Mm. So this was one of the foundational questions when we decided to embark on this work, is what does this look like? Now we know it's 94,000 people approximately.

And- Yeah. And I think one of the key messages from this OTEP is how invisible this dual population is- Yeah... within data- Yeah... within existing research. Yeah. And that's primarily because of the mechanisms we are collecting and using data- Yeah... in the sense that, whether it be prioritized ethnicity or that you have to, in your interactions with any government agencies- Mm... or their service providers, you have to opt for one ethnicity.

It's diluting- Mm... the multi-ethnicity component. Mm. So when you start asking about specificities or sub-ethnicity with tribes or hapū, it's just going to get more and more diluted from an aggregate view. It's already- Yeah... poorer data. And we are conscious that, with moving to a new census, and that's with the existing census, by the way.

Moving to a new census base is going to put pressures on still how we collect ethnicity data. And it's something that we are often advocating for to ensure that we have good, strong ethnicity data, so that we can understand identity, but also understand drivers behind need as well. Mm. Mm. Sure. I was particularly interested in a lot of the comments around the education system, and we've just had the Waitangi Tribunal inquiring into changes to the curriculum-... both the NZC curriculum and Te Marautanga.

And I can see some of the same issues that are being raised there are coming up for Pasifika children. So for Maori, 97% of tamariki Maori are in English medium education. Mm. How important it is to have within that curriculum and within that system, that sense of identity, being able to see themselves in what they're being taught, and yeah, the learning that's happening there.

It also makes me think about from our ECE level, and I know that we have some Pasifika ECE. Mm. We are oversubscribed in kōhanga reo and a need for that to grow. You're talking about a really young demographic here. Is there more that you could expand on in terms of that education, not only in the language that they're saying they want to be learning in both languages, but also through into the compulsory education sector?

In the first instance, we'd probably use this document to talk about some of the perspectives with the Ministry of Education. What I have been excited by recently, and I don't know if you've had the opportunity to visit the Sisters United Academy, this is a charter school operating out of the Alexandra Raceway. Already it has over 100 women in years nine and year 10, formative years, where they are led by Pasifika teachers, Pasifika

kaupapa, and they've worked on the opening for the first term as well as the academic achievements. But the strength of their identity was really significant, and the safety and pastoral care that was offered, that was visible between the teachers and their students, made me feel a little bit jealous that I didn't get the opportunity-... to participate in an education program like that myself. Not that my parents would've let me do that because-

... they probably would've seen it as too much performance and not enough academic. But for this cohort, I could see really visible, strong sense of- Mm... identity and I would have no doubt that this would translate into academic achievement.

Mm. So I can't answer the wider question- Mm... but to point you to places where you see this thriving, and I would recommend a visit to the Sisters United Academy. And I suppose that highlights perhaps an issue with our English medium schooling system, where that isn't provided there. And so you have a community looking at either a charter school or a special character school to be able to respond to that need.

With early childhood education, it's complex because early childhood education in language nests, often the focus is on preservation of the language, and that's amazing. And sometimes the early childhood education bit becomes secondary to the language. So it offers a place for often our matua to find employment and to speak the language. But then they put all the kids to sleep at the same time, and they may have practices that wouldn't be seen by some

as being up with early childhood, good early childhood education practice. Conversely, then you get a center that has really good early childhood education and absolutely no ability to harness the language, and I think this will be a policy dilemma for us as parents' needs in some areas. The cohort that we're talking about, early parents, the 15-year-olds, the 16-year-olds, they are already parents. And this is not to judge them.

Often they come from parents who are-- I interviewed some and was like, "Oh, so I would be the same age as your parent." And then it dawned on me that I'm the same age as their grandparent and bordering on great-grandparent. So these are people that want their children to align to a strong identity, and one that accommodates for the very diverse needs of our community. Mm.

Yeah. Might I add, I do think there is a desire for culturally appropriate responses in education. It's just going to be a bit more difficult because it's not one culture, it's not Maori or Pacific, it is Maori and Pacific. Mm-hmm. So when you're talking about bilingualism, how do you incorporate that in schools? How do you incorporate which values? So it's just a discussion that we will bring attention to our counterparts in education.

Mm. Mm. I suppose to build on it, it's not just, what would they say? Two cultures, but it's multiple cultures, multiple languages- Yeah... and the resourcing of that sort of thing. That's right. There's a quote in the report- And dialects. Yes. There's a quote in the report that says, "I don't want to just identify one ethnicity. What about the other seven?"

And so it summarizes it pretty well. Might I add as well, it is a strength. I think countries or jurisdictions that embrace cross-cultural capability actually is a strength. Research shows that there is a strong correlation to greater innovation, greater trust- Yeah ... greater productivity. Yes. So it's more about how we embrace it as a positive thing to incorporate.

Oh, yeah. I can see it amongst our athletes with dual whakapapa. Many of them are influencers, and you can just see it. They are absolutely thriving in all of their whakapapa. Beautiful to see. Yeah. Yeah. I think we've seemed to have our questioning phase is sort of drawing to a close, but are there any other sort of key points that we haven't touched on in the oral hearing that you'd like to emphasize?

Thank you for your openness to listening to our Long-term Insights briefing and the opportunity to bring some of that to life. Oh, sorry. I do have one more. Go for it. We do have one more. Go for it. Sorry, everybody. We had this briefing this morning from the Helen Clark Foundation, and I know you've talked about a particularly young cohort. Mm. But they were talking- Yeah ...

about a lot of the challenges that we've got with our aging population. And given my own experience with whanau who whakapapa, and this is back to four great-grandparents, so there are some quite elderly ones in that. They talked about the provision of care- Mm ...

and what Maori and Pasifika tend to not go into retirement villages, rest homes, they can be more in community, in whanau. Was there anything that you heard around that, or did you just focus on the younger group? I think in relation to that report, there are two things that really stand out as really important. The drop in trust in government, and how do we work towards that, and the issues around social cohesion, which do include the intergenerational transfer of knowledge and care.

We know that Pasifika and Maori tend to look after their elderly. If they stop doing that, there's going to be a significant cost to our country. Mm. And largely because it's not these people that are going to be elderly. Mm. Mm. I have a supplementary, that's all.

Okay. Yes. So one of the things that we talked about, and was emphasized in that report, was that we need a lot more small houses for elderly people, and it was something I thought about at the time, was isn't that a little bit culturally narrow? Like, a lot of our communities actually share housing, and it is very lonely being in all these little houses. And so when we are planning in that way for housing for the elderly, do you think we have to be cognizant of a whole lot of cultures which have kind of got it right,

and they do actually need bigger spaces and more connection with each other, and they need to be intergenerational? Mm.That's such a big question. I know. I'm sorry. One minute to answer. It's been an interesting day. Yeah. Yes. And I'm reluctant to answer it, because I will give an answer that doesn't do justice to the complexity of the issue- Yeah. Love the answer... particularly because of the dynamics of the community.

So I suppose then from us, something to pick up from those two reports is that connection around it, and I agree with Helen. I was kind of like, "Oh, smaller, single, but what about intergenerational?" And so then we're actually talking about larger. And I know that some of our state houses have been built and provide for that. So, they have their own space, but they're part of a bigger one.

But yeah, where is that need? How do we plan for that? What does that spread look like across the country? So maybe something to look into how this report feeds into that strategy that needs to be developed. If you get a chance, I would recommend having a look at the Our Whare, Our Whanau project, which has been based and designed around a Pacific village concept. So the houses are designed with multiple adaptable spaces, and it is very much like a village with a shared- Where's that?

... family space. In Porirua. Oh, in Porirua. In Porirua, yeah. The school's also like that, isn't it? The college is- Yes... based around a whanau system. Yes. Might I add just one point, just in relation to universal solutions don't apply. They're not useful in general. So to be more targeted around service design or whatever you're offering in terms of the production of houses, you do need to take into account just how people behave, and that's driven by ethnicities and it's driven by a geography here. So definitely one size fits all would

not be an effective way to design a solution. One last question from Wharerangi. Yes. And it's just probably from what Willow Jean was saying in the select committee briefing that we had this morning. Did in any of your conversations with this young cohort, did they talk about their...

You talked about embracing wisdom, and I'm quite sure that is so. Did they talk about their old ones? Did they talk about how they saw their old ones, and what do they think of their old ones? Did they talk about them? Did they see what they envisaged for their old ones, their older whanau, older whanau?
